9.ESV:
1. Now it is superfluous for me to write to you about the ministry for the saints,
2. for I know your readiness, of which I boast about you to the people of Macedonia, saying that Achaia has been ready since last year. And your zeal has stirred up most of them.
3. But I am sending the brothers so that our boasting about you may not prove empty in this matter, so that you may be ready, as I said you would be.
4. Otherwise, if some Macedonians come with me and find that you are not ready, we would be humiliated—to say nothing of you—for being so confident.
5. So I thought it necessary to urge the brothers to go on ahead to you and arrange in advance for the gift you have promised, so that it may be ready as a willing gift, not as an exaction.
6. The point is this: whoever sows sparingly will also reap sparingly, and whoever sows bountifully will also reap bountifully.
7. Each one must give as he has decided in his heart, not reluctantly or under compulsion, for God loves a cheerful giver.
8. And God is able to make all grace abound to you, so that having all sufficiency in all things at all times, you may abound in every good work.
9. As it is written, “He has distributed freely, he has given to the poor; his righteousness endures forever.”
10. He who supplies seed to the sower and bread for food will supply and multiply your seed for sowing and increase the harvest of your righteousness.
11. You will be enriched in every way to be generous in every way, which through us will produce thanksgiving to God.
12. For the ministry of this service is not only supplying the needs of the saints but is also overflowing in many thanksgivings to God.
13. By their approval of this service, they will glorify God because of your submission that comes from your confession of the gospel of Christ, and the generosity of your contribution for them and for all others,
14. while they long for you and pray for you, because of the surpassing grace of God upon you.
15. Thanks be to God for his inexpressible gift!

3. Yet have I sent the brethren, lest our boasting of you should be in vain in this behalf; that, as I said, ye may be ready:

9.notes:
2 Corinthians - Chapter 9
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Paul teaches on cheerful, generous giving — "God loves a cheerful giver" — promising that God is able to make all grace abound so that the giver always has enough for every good work, and generous giving produces thanksgiving to God.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Paul (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 9 continues the teaching on generous giving —
containing some of the most quoted verses on Christian stewardship. Paul
encourages the Corinthians to follow through on their pledge, using agricultural
metaphors (sowing and reaping) and emphasizing God's ability to supply all needs.

The Sowing Principle (v.6): "He which soweth sparingly shall reap also sparingly;
and he which soweth bountifully shall reap also bountifully." Giving is like
sowing seed — the harvest is proportional to the sowing. Generous giving
produces generous return — not necessarily financial but in every form of grace.

The Cheerful Giver (v.7): "God loveth a cheerful giver." One of the most
famous verses on giving. Three principles: (1) give as you have purposed in
your heart (planned, not impulsive), (2) not grudgingly or of necessity (willing,
not pressured), (3) cheerfully (joyfully, not reluctantly). God loves the
attitude more than the amount.

God's Sufficiency (v.8): "God is able to make all grace abound toward you; that
ye, always having all sufficiency in all things, may abound to every good work."
Five "alls" — God's provision is comprehensive. He gives so you can give.

"His Unspeakable Gift" (v.15): The chapter ends with Christ — "Thanks be unto
God for his unspeakable gift." Every act of human generosity is a response to
God's indescribable generosity in giving His Son.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book           | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|----------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.6              | Galatians      | 6       | 7                  | "A man reaps what he sows."
v.6              | Proverbs       | 11      | 24                 | "One person gives freely, yet gains even more."
v.7              | Exodus         | 25      | 2                  | "Everyone whose heart prompts them to give."
v.7              | Romans         | 12      | 8                  | "If it is giving, then give generously."
v.8              | Philippians    | 4       | 19                 | "My God will meet all your needs."
v.8              | Ephesians      | 3       | 20                 | "Able to do immeasurably more than all we ask."
v.9              | Psalm          | 112     | 9                  | "They have freely scattered their gifts to the poor."
v.10             | Isaiah         | 55      | 10                 | "As the rain and the snow come down from heaven."
v.10             | Hosea          | 10      | 12                 | "Sow righteousness for yourselves, reap the fruit of
v.11             | 2 Corinthians  | 8       | 2                  | "Their overflowing joy... welled up in rich
v.12             | Philippians    | 4       | 18                 | "A fragrant offering, an acceptable sacrifice."
v.13             | Matthew        | 5       | 16                 | "Let your light shine before others."
v.15             | John           | 3       | 16                 | "God so loved the world that he gave."
v.15             | Romans         | 8       | 32                 | "He who did not spare his own Son."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/2-corinthians-9/
  Guzik examines the sowing principle, the cheerful giver, God's
  sufficiency, and the unspeakable gift.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "The Cheerful Giver" on v.7 (MTP Vol. 10, No. 667)
  "God loveth a cheerful giver — not a grudging giver, not a pressured
  giver, not a guilty giver — a CHEERFUL giver. The Greek is 'hilaron' —
  hilarious. God loves the giver who gives with joy, with delight, with
  enthusiasm. If you can't give cheerfully, work on your heart before you
  work on your wallet."
  Sermon: "The Unspeakable Gift" on v.15 (MTP Vol. 22, No. 1396)
  "Thanks be unto God for His unspeakable gift — Christ. Unspeakable —
  beyond words, beyond description, beyond comprehension. Every act of
  human generosity is a faint echo of God's generosity in giving His Son.
  You will never out-give God."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.6              | KJV         | "bountifully"
v.6              | ESV         | Both equivalent. — Greek "ep' eulogiais" — literally "upon blessings." Sow with blessings and you reap blessings. The harvest matches the sowing.
v.6              | NET         | "the person who sows generously will also reap generously" — "generously" (ep' eulogiais = upon blessings). WEB: "He who sows bountifully will also reap bountifully." KJV: "He which soweth bountifully shall reap also bountifully." Agricultural law applied to giving — the harvest matches the sowing.
v.7              | KJV         | "cheerful"
v.7              | ESV         | Both equivalent. — Greek "hilaron" — joyful, glad, hilarious. The source of our word "hilarious." God loves giving that is done with genuine joy.
v.7              | KJV         | "purposeth in his heart"
v.7              | ESV         | "give as he has decided in his heart" — Planned, deliberate, decided in advance. Not impulsive or pressured but thoughtful and intentional.
v.7              | NET         | "Each one of you should give just as he has decided in his heart, not reluctantly or under compulsion, because God loves a cheerful giver" — "cheerful" (hilaron = joyful/ glad — English "hilarious" derives from this). WEB: "God loves a cheerful giver." Compelled giving contradicts grace.
v.8              | KJV         | "all grace... all sufficiency... all things... every good work"
v.8              | ESV         | "grace abound to you, so that having all sufficiency in all" — Five comprehensive terms. God's provision is total — He gives everything needed for everything He calls you to do.
v.8              | NET         | "And God is able to make all grace overflow to you so that because you always have enough of everything, you may overflow in every good work" — "all grace... always... enough of everything... every good work" — comprehensive provision. WEB: "God is able to make all grace abound to you." Grace enables generosity in a cycle.
v.10             | NET         | "will supply and multiply your seed for sowing and will cause the harvest of your righteousness to grow" — quoting Isaiah 55:10. WEB: "multiply your seed for sowing, and increase the fruits of your righteousness." God supplies the resources to be generous — He funds what He commands.
v.15             | KJV         | "unspeakable gift"
v.15             | ESV         | "his inexpressible gift!" — Greek "anekdiēgētō" — indescribable, beyond words. Christ is a gift so great that language cannot capture it.
v.15             | NET         | "Thanks be to God for his indescribable gift!" — "indescribable" (anekdiEgEtO = beyond words/inexpressible). WEB: "Thanks be to God for his unspeakable gift!" KJV: "Thanks be unto God for his unspeakable gift." The ultimate gift (Christ) makes all other giving possible.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                   | Notes
------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------|------
Sowing/Reaping    | The agricultural principle applied to giving: what you plant determines what you harvest. Generous sowing produces generous reaping. | 
Cheerful giver    | Greek "hilaron" — joyful, glad. God values the attitude of giving more than the amount. Reluctant giving dishonors God. | 
All sufficiency   | God's comprehensive provision — enough for your needs AND enough to give generously to others. | 
Unspeakable gift  | Christ — God's indescribable, incomprehensible gift to humanity. The ultimate act of generosity that motivates all human giving. | 
Seed to the sower | God provides both for your personal needs (bread) and for your giving (seed). He supplies the resources for generosity. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "He which soweth bountifully shall reap also bountifully" (v.6). Your
   giving is seed — it produces a harvest. Are you sowing sparingly or
   bountifully? What harvest are you expecting?

2. "God loveth a cheerful giver" (v.7). How do you feel when you give — joyful
   or reluctant? If giving feels like a burden, the issue is your heart, not
   your wallet. What would cheerful giving look like for you?

3. "God is able to make all grace abound toward you" (v.8). You cannot out-give
   God. He provides so you can give — and then provides more. Do you trust
   God's ability to supply your needs if you give generously?

4. "Thanks be unto God for his unspeakable gift" (v.15). All human generosity
   is a response to God's generosity in Christ. When you give, you are
   imitating the God who gave His Son. How does Christ's gift motivate yours?
5. Paul says give "as he purposeth in his heart" (v.7) — planned, not
   impulsive. Do you plan your giving in advance, or is it random and
   reactive? What would intentional, planned generosity look like?

6. "Not grudgingly, or of necessity" (v.7). If you give out of guilt or
   pressure, God is not pleased — even if the amount is large. What motivates
   your giving — joy or obligation?

7. God provides "seed to the sower" (v.10) — He gives you resources
   specifically for giving. Some of what God gives you is not for you — it's
   seed to be sown. How much of your income do you view as "seed for sowing"
   vs. "bread for eating"?

8. Generous giving produces thanksgiving to God (v.12) and glorifies Him (v.13).
   Your generosity doesn't just meet needs — it produces worship. How does
   knowing that your giving causes others to praise God motivate you?

9. The chapter ends with Christ — "his unspeakable gift" (v.15). Everything
   comes back to the Gospel. God gave His Son — the greatest gift imaginable.
   How does the cross shape your understanding of generosity?

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — 2 Corinthians Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/2-corinthians/

David Guzik — 2 Corinthians 9 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/2-corinthians-9/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

10.notes:
2 Corinthians - Chapter 10
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Paul defends his apostolic authority against challengers, declaring that the weapons of his warfare are not fleshly but divinely powerful — "casting down imaginations, and every high thing that exalteth itself against the knowledge of God."

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Paul (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapters 10-13 form the most intense section of 2 Corinthians
— Paul's defense of his apostleship against false teachers ("super-apostles")
who had infiltrated Corinth. These chapters are passionate, personal, and
sometimes sarcastic. Paul defends not for his own sake but for the Gospel's sake.

Spiritual Warfare (vv.3-5): One of the most important passages on spiritual
warfare in the NT. "The weapons of our warfare are not carnal, but mighty
through God" (v.4). The battle is for the mind — "casting down imaginations,
and every high thing that exalteth itself against the knowledge of God, and
bringing into captivity every thought to the obedience of Christ" (v.5).

The Accusation (v.10): Paul's critics said "his letters are weighty and powerful;
but his bodily presence is weak, and his speech contemptible." They judged by
appearance — Paul was unimpressive physically and rhetorically. Paul's response:
what I am in letters, I will be in person (v.11).

"He that glorieth, let him glory in the Lord" (v.17): The chapter's conclusion
— echoing 1 Corinthians 1:31. The only legitimate boast is the Lord. "Not he
that commendeth himself is approved, but whom the Lord commendeth" (v.18).

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book           | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|----------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Matthew        | 11      | 29                 | "I am gentle and humble in heart."
v.3              | Ephesians      | 6       | 12                 | "Our struggle is not against flesh and blood."
v.4              | Ephesians      | 6       | 13-17              | The armor of God

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/2-corinthians-10/
  Guzik examines spiritual warfare, the weapons of the mind, Paul's
  defense against critics, and the danger of self-comparison.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "Spiritual Warfare" on vv.4-5 (MTP Vol. 10, No. 668)
  "The weapons of our warfare are not carnal — not human eloquence, not
  political power, not social pressure — but mighty through God. The battle
  is for the mind. Every thought must be captured and brought to Christ.
  Every imagination that exalts itself against God must be demolished."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.3              | NET         | "the weapons of our warfare are not human weapons, but are made powerful by God for tearing down strongholds" — "strongholds" (ochyrOmatOn = fortifications). WEB: "the weapons of our warfare are not of the flesh, but mighty before God to the throwing down of strongholds." Spiritual warfare requires spiritual weapons.
v.4              | KJV         | "mighty through God"
v.4              | ESV         | "of the flesh but have divine" — Greek "dunata tō theō" — powerful in God, divinely powerful. Not human strength but God's power working through spiritual weapons.
v.4              | NET         | "We tear down arguments" — "arguments" (logismous = reasonings/speculations). WEB: "throwing down imaginations." KJV: "Casting down imaginations." The battlefield is the mind — false ideologies and human reasoning opposed to God.
v.5              | KJV         | "imaginations"
v.5              | ESV         | "We destroy arguments and" — Greek "logismous" — reasonings, arguments, thought patterns. The battle is intellectual/spiritual — wrong thinking must be demolished and replaced with Christ's truth.
v.5              | KJV         | "bringing into captivity every thought"
v.5              | ESV         | "of God, and take every thought captive" — Military language: taking prisoners of war. Every thought that rebels against Christ must be captured and made obedient.
v.5              | NET         | "and every arrogant obstacle that is raised up against the knowledge of God, and we take every thought captive to make it obey Christ" — "every thought captive" (pan noEma aichmalOtizontes). WEB: "bringing every thought into captivity to the obedience of Christ." Comprehensive mental discipline — no thought exempt from Christ's lordship.
v.10             | NET         | "His letters are weighty and powerful, but his physical presence is weak and his speech is of no account" — the opponents' critique of Paul. WEB: "his bodily presence is weak, and his speech is despised." Paul may have had physical ailments and lacked rhetorical polish.
v.12             | KJV         | "are not wise"
v.12             | ESV         | "without understanding" — Understatement. Comparing yourself to others (rather than to God's standard) is foolish — it always produces either pride or despair.
v.12             | NET         | "we do not dare to classify or compare ourselves with some of those who recommend themselves" — "compare ourselves" (synkrinai heautous) — measuring ministry by comparison is foolish. WEB: "comparing themselves with themselves." Self- referential metrics produce self-deception.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                   | Notes
------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------|------
Spiritual warfare | The battle against spiritual forces using spiritual weapons — prayer, Scripture, truth, faith. Not physical but real. | 
Strongholds       | Fortified positions of wrong thinking — deeply entrenched lies, arguments, and thought patterns that oppose God's truth. | 
Imaginations      | Greek "logismoi" — reasonings, arguments, philosophies that exalt themselves against the knowledge of God. | 
Captive thoughts  | Every thought brought under Christ's authority — not allowed to roam free but submitted to His lordship. | 
Carnal weapons    | Human methods — eloquence, manipulation, political power, social pressure. Ineffective against spiritual strongholds. | 
Self-commendation | Praising yourself. Paul says it's worthless — only God's commendation matters. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "The weapons of our warfare are not carnal" (v.4). You cannot fight
   spiritual battles with human weapons. Prayer, Scripture, truth, and faith
   are your weapons. Are you using spiritual weapons or relying on human
   strategies?

2. "Bringing into captivity every thought to the obedience of Christ" (v.5).
   The battle is in your mind — every thought must be captured and submitted
   to Christ. What thoughts are running free in your mind that need to be
   captured? Anxiety? Lust? Bitterness? Lies?

3. "They measuring themselves by themselves... are not wise" (v.12). Comparing
   yourself to others always distorts reality. Are you comparing yourself to
   other men — producing either pride or discouragement? The only standard
   is Christ.

4. "Not he that commendeth himself is approved, but whom the Lord commendeth"
   (v.18). Whose approval are you seeking — your own, others', or God's?
   Only God's commendation matters eternally.

11.notes:
2 Corinthians - Chapter 11
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Paul reluctantly boasts as a "fool," warning against false apostles disguised as angels of light, and catalogs his unmatched sufferings — beatings, shipwrecks, hunger, danger, and the daily pressure of concern for all the churches.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Paul (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 11 is Paul's "fool's speech" — reluctantly boasting
to counter the false apostles who had impressed Corinth with their credentials.
Paul's "boast" is unique: he boasts in his sufferings (vv.23-33), not his
successes. His credentials are scars, not diplomas.

False Apostles (vv.13-15): Paul identifies his opponents as "false apostles,
deceitful workers, transforming themselves into the apostles of Christ" (v.13).
Their master: "Satan himself is transformed into an angel of light" (v.14).
False teachers look good — that's what makes them dangerous.

Paul's Suffering Catalog (vv.23-28): The most detailed list of Paul's sufferings
in the NT: beatings, imprisonments, near-death experiences, 39 lashes (five
times), beaten with rods (three times), stoned, shipwrecked (three times),
constant danger, hunger, cold, and "the care of all the churches" (v.28). These
are his credentials — not eloquence or impressiveness.

"Who is weak, and I am not weak?" (v.29): Paul's final credential: empathy.
He feels the weakness of every believer. He burns with indignation when anyone
is led astray. His authority flows from love, not power.

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/2-corinthians-11/
  Guzik examines the false apostles, Satan's disguise, Paul's suffering
  catalog, and his boasting in weakness.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "An Angel of Light" on v.14 (MTP Vol. 10, No. 669)
  "Satan himself is transformed into an angel of light — he doesn't come
  with horns and a pitchfork. He comes looking good, sounding right,
  appearing spiritual. That is his most effective disguise. The most
  dangerous false teachers look the most impressive."
  Sermon: "Paul's Sufferings" on vv.23-28 (MTP Vol. 22, No. 1397)
  "In labours more abundant, in stripes above measure, in prisons more
  frequent — these are Paul's credentials. Not his degrees, not his
  eloquence, not his success — his scars. The marks of genuine ministry
  are not comfort and applause but suffering and faithfulness."

v.2              | NET         | "I am jealous for you with godly jealousy, because I promised you in marriage to one husband, to present you as a pure virgin to Christ" — "godly jealousy" (theou zElO = God's own jealousy). WEB: "a godly jealousy." Paul as spiritual father protecting his daughter for her Bridegroom.
v.3              | KJV         | "the simplicity that is in Christ"
v.3              | ESV         | "a sincere and pure devotion to Christ" — Greek "haplotētos" — simplicity, sincerity, single-mindedness. The Gospel is simple — false teachers complicate it.
v.4              | NET         | "For if someone comes and proclaims another Jesus than the one we proclaimed" — "another Jesus" (allon IEsoun) — a counterfeit Christ. WEB: "preaches another Jesus." False teaching doesn't deny Jesus — it redefines Him.
v.14             | KJV         | "an angel of light"
v.14             | ESV         | Both equivalent. — Satan's most effective disguise is not ugliness but beauty. He appears as something good, spiritual, and attractive.
v.14             | NET         | "And no wonder, for even Satan disguises himself as an angel of light" — "disguises himself" (metaschEmatizesthai = transforms his appearance). WEB: "even Satan transforms himself into an angel of light." KJV: "Satan himself is transformed into an angel of light." The most dangerous deception looks most spiritual.
v.24             | KJV         | "forty stripes save one"
v.24             | ESV         | "of the Jews the forty lashes less one" — Jewish law limited flogging to 40 lashes (Deuteronomy 25:3). They gave 39 to avoid accidentally exceeding the limit. Paul received this FIVE times.
v.24             | NET         | "Five times I received from the Jews forty lashes less one" — 39 lashes (the maximum without risking death). WEB: "Five times from the Jews I received forty stripes minus one." Paul endured this five times — 195 lashes total. None of these are recorded in Acts.
v.28             | KJV         | "the care of all the churches"
v.28             | ESV         | "me of my anxiety for all the churches" — Greek "merimna" — anxiety, concern, burden. Paul carried the weight of every church's problems daily — perhaps his heaviest burden.
v.28             | NET         | "Apart from other things, there is the daily pressure on me of my anxiety for all the churches" — "daily pressure" (epistasis... kath' hEmeran). WEB: "that which presses on me daily: anxiety for all the assemblies." The heaviest burden isn't physical — it's pastoral concern.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term                     | Definition                                                   | Notes
-------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------|------
False apostles           | Deceitful workers disguised as Christ's apostles. They looked impressive but served Satan's purposes. | 
Angel of light           | Satan's disguise — appearing good, spiritual, and attractive. His ministers also disguise themselves as righteous. | 
Forty stripes save one   | 39 lashes — the maximum Jewish flogging. Paul received this five times — 195 lashes total. | 
Care of all the churches | Paul's daily burden: the spiritual welfare of every church he had planted. Emotional and spiritual weight. | 
Simplicity in Christ     | The straightforward, uncomplicated Gospel. False teachers add complexity; the truth is simple. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Satan himself is transformed into an angel of light" (v.14). The most
   dangerous deception looks good. How do you evaluate teachers and leaders —
   by appearance and impressiveness, or by faithfulness to the Gospel?

2. Paul's credentials were sufferings (vv.23-28), not successes. How do you
   evaluate ministry — by comfort and growth, or by faithfulness under
   pressure? What does this say about how you evaluate your own life?

3. "Who is weak, and I am not weak?" (v.29). Paul felt every believer's
   weakness as his own. How empathetic are you toward struggling believers?
   Do you feel their pain or judge their weakness?

4. "I will glory of the things which concern mine infirmities" (v.30). Paul
   boasts in weakness — the opposite of the world's approach. What would it
   look like to boast in your weaknesses rather than hide them?

12.notes:
2 Corinthians - Chapter 12
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Paul recounts being caught up to the third heaven (paradise), reveals his thorn in the flesh given to keep him humble, and receives God's sufficient answer: "My grace is sufficient for thee: for my strength is made perfect in weakness."

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Paul (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 12 contains two of the most significant passages
in Paul's letters: his vision of paradise (vv.1-6) and his "thorn in the flesh"
(vv.7-10). Both reveal the paradox of Paul's life — extraordinary spiritual
experiences AND extraordinary suffering. God's answer to the thorn: "My grace
is sufficient for thee: for my strength is made perfect in weakness" (v.9).

Paul's Vision (vv.1-6): Fourteen years earlier, Paul was "caught up to the
third heaven" (v.2) — paradise — and "heard unspeakable words" (v.4). He
speaks of himself in the third person ("I knew a man," v.2) — reluctant to
boast even about genuine spiritual experiences.

The Thorn in the Flesh (vv.7-10): "A thorn in the flesh, the messenger of
Satan to buffet me" (v.7). Its identity is unknown — guesses include: eye
disease, malaria, epilepsy, a speech impediment, or persistent opposition.
Paul prayed three times for its removal. God said no — and gave something
better: "My grace is sufficient for thee" (v.9).

"When I Am Weak, Then Am I Strong" (v.10): The chapter's climax and one of
the most counterintuitive statements in Scripture. Strength through weakness.
Power through frailty. God's strength displayed through human inability. This
is the opposite of everything the world teaches about success.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book           | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|----------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.2              | Acts           | 14      | 19-20              | Possibly when Paul was stoned and left for dead
v.2              | Revelation     | 2       | 7                  | "Paradise" — the presence of God
v.4              | Revelation     | 10      | 4                  | "Seal up what the seven thunders have said."
v.7              | Job            | 2       | 6                  | Satan given permission to afflict
v.7              | Numbers        | 33      | 55                 | "Thorns in your sides."
v.8              | Matthew        | 26      | 39                 | ,42,44 — Jesus praying three times in Gethsemane
v.9              | 2 Corinthians  | 3       | 5                  | "Our sufficiency is of God."
v.9              | Philippians    | 4       | 13                 | "I can do all things through Christ."
v.9              | 2 Corinthians  | 4       | 7                  | "Treasure in earthen vessels."
v.10             | 2 Corinthians  | 4       | 8-9                | "Troubled... yet not distressed."
v.10             | 2 Corinthians  | 13      | 4                  | "He was crucified in weakness, yet he lives by
v.12             | Romans         | 15      | 19                 | "By the power of signs and wonders."
v.14             | 1 Thessalonians | 2       | 9                  | "We worked night and day."
v.15             | Philippians    | 2       | 17                 | "Being poured out like a drink offering."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/2-corinthians-12/
  Guzik examines the paradise vision, the thorn's identity and purpose,
  God's sufficient grace, and strength through weakness.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "My Grace Is Sufficient" on v.9 (MTP Vol. 10, No. 670)
  "My grace is sufficient for thee — not 'will be' but 'IS.' Present tense.
  Right now. In this moment. Whatever you face — the grace is already there.
  Sufficient — not barely adequate but fully sufficient. And His strength
  is made perfect in weakness — not despite weakness but THROUGH it. Your
  weakness is the canvas on which God paints His power."
  Sermon: "When I Am Weak" on v.10 (MTP Vol. 22, No. 1398)
  "When I am weak, then am I strong — the world says 'be strong.' God says
  'be weak — and I will be strong in you.' The man who admits his weakness
  receives God's strength. The man who insists on his own strength never
  experiences God's."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "the third heaven"
v.2              | ESV         | Both equivalent. — Jewish cosmology: (1) atmosphere, (2) space/ stars, (3) God's dwelling place. Paul was taken to God's immediate presence — paradise.
v.2              | NET         | "I know a man in Christ who fourteen years ago... was caught up to the third heaven" — "the third heaven" = God's presence (first = atmosphere, second = space, third = God's dwelling). WEB: "caught up into the third heaven." Paul speaks of himself in third person out of humility.
v.4              | NET         | "was caught up into paradise and heard things too sacred to be put into words" — "too sacred" (arrhEta rhEmata = inexpressible utterances). WEB: "unspeakable words." KJV: "unspeakable words, which it is not lawful for a man to utter." Some revelations are meant only for the recipient.
v.7              | KJV         | "a thorn in the flesh"
v.7              | ESV         | "a thorn was given me in the flesh" — Greek "skolops" — a stake or thorn. Something painful, persistent, and humbling. Its specific identity is deliberately left unstated — so every suffering believer can relate.
v.7              | KJV         | "the messenger of Satan"
v.7              | ESV         | "the flesh, a messenger of Satan" — The thorn was satanic in origin but divine in purpose. God used Satan's attack to keep Paul humble.
v.7              | NET         | "a thorn in the flesh was given to me, a messenger of Satan to trouble me" — "thorn" (skolops = stake/splinter). WEB: "a thorn in the flesh." KJV: "a thorn in the flesh." Its exact nature is unknown — theories include: eye disease, malaria, epilepsy, opposition, or a speech impediment.
v.9              | KJV         | "My grace is sufficient"
v.9              | ESV         | Both equivalent. — Greek "arkei" — enough, adequate, satisfying. Not "My grace will eventually be sufficient" but "IS sufficient" — present, active, available now.
v.9              | KJV         | "made perfect in weakness"
v.9              | ESV         | Both equivalent. — Greek "teleitai" — completed, brought to full expression. God's power reaches its fullest display in human weakness.
v.9              | NET         | "My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness" — "made perfect" (teleitai = completed/reaches its goal). WEB: "My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness." KJV: "My grace is sufficient for thee: for my strength is made perfect in weakness." God's answer to Paul's three requests.
v.10             | NET         | "when I am weak, then I am strong" — the great paradox. WEB: "when I am weak, then am I strong." KJV: "when I am weak, then am I strong." Human weakness becomes the venue for divine power — not despite weakness but THROUGH it.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term                 | Definition                                                   | Notes
---------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------|------
Third heaven         | God's dwelling place — paradise. Beyond the atmosphere and space. Paul was taken there in a vision/experience. | 
Thorn in the flesh   | A persistent, painful affliction given to keep Paul humble. Its identity is unknown — deliberately, so all suffering believers can identify. | 
Messenger of Satan   | The thorn's origin: satanic. But its purpose: divine (preventing pride). God uses even Satan's attacks for His purposes. | 
Sufficient grace     | God's grace as fully adequate for every situation — not removing the thorn but providing strength to endure it. | 
Strength in weakness | The paradox: God's power is most fully displayed through human inability. Weakness is the condition for experiencing divine strength. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "My grace is sufficient for thee" (v.9). God said NO to Paul's prayer —
   and gave something better than removal: sufficiency. Is there a prayer God
   has said "no" to? Could His grace be sufficient for you IN the situation
   rather than OUT of it?

2. "My strength is made perfect in weakness" (v.9). God's power is displayed
   most fully through your weakness — not your strength. Where are you weakest?
   That may be where God's power is most available. Can you stop hiding your
   weakness and let God work through it?

3. "When I am weak, then am I strong" (v.10). The world says "be strong."
   God says "admit your weakness and receive My strength." Which approach are
   you taking — self-sufficiency or God-dependence?

4. Paul prayed three times for the thorn's removal (v.8). God said no. Not
   every prayer is answered with "yes." Sometimes God's "no" is better than
   your "yes." Can you trust God's "no" as much as His "yes"?
5. Paul had extraordinary spiritual experiences (vv.1-4) AND extraordinary
   suffering (v.7). Both were part of God's plan. How do you hold together
   spiritual highs and persistent pain in your own life?

6. The thorn was given "lest I should be exalted above measure" (v.7). God
   uses suffering to prevent pride. What in your life keeps you humble? Could
   your "thorn" be God's protection against pride?

7. Paul "besought the Lord thrice" (v.8) — then accepted God's answer. He
   didn't keep begging indefinitely. When do you stop asking and start
   accepting? How do you know when God's answer is final?

8. "I take pleasure in infirmities" (v.10). Paul didn't just endure weakness
   — he took PLEASURE in it. How? Because weakness was the condition for
   experiencing Christ's power. Can you reach that level of trust?

9. God's grace is "sufficient" (v.9) — not "barely adequate" but fully
   satisfying. In your current struggle, is God's grace enough? Or are you
   demanding that He remove the problem instead of sustaining you through it?

13.notes:
2 Corinthians - Chapter 13
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Paul warns he will not spare the disobedient on his third visit, urges self-examination ("Examine yourselves, whether ye be in the faith"), and closes with the Trinitarian benediction: "The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, and the love of God, and the communion of the Holy Ghost, be with you all."

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Paul (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 13 is Paul's closing — a final warning (vv.1-4),
a call to self-examination (vv.5-6), a prayer for their perfection (vv.7-10),
and a benediction (vv.11-14). The letter ends with one of the most beautiful
Trinitarian blessings in Scripture (v.14).

The Call to Self-Examination (v.5): "Examine yourselves, whether ye be in the
faith; prove your own selves." Instead of examining Paul (demanding proof of
his apostleship), they should examine themselves. The question is not "Is Paul
legitimate?" but "Am I genuinely in the faith?"

The Trinitarian Benediction (v.14): "The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, and
the love of God, and the communion of the Holy Ghost, be with you all." The
most complete Trinitarian blessing in the NT — grace from Christ, love from
the Father, fellowship from the Spirit. Three persons, one God, three distinct
gifts.

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/2-corinthians-13/
  Guzik examines the warning, self-examination, the prayer for perfection,
  and the Trinitarian benediction.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "Examine Yourselves" on v.5 (MTP Vol. 10, No. 671)
  "Examine yourselves — not your neighbor, not your pastor, not your church
  — yourselves. Are YOU in the faith? Is Christ in YOU? Don't assume. Don't
  coast on past experience. Examine. Prove. Know. The unexamined faith may
  not be faith at all."
  Sermon: "The Trinitarian Blessing" on v.14 (MTP Vol. 22, No. 1399)
  "The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ — grace from the Son who became poor
  for you. The love of God — love from the Father who gave His Son. The
  communion of the Holy Ghost — fellowship with the Spirit who dwells in
  you. Three persons. Three gifts. One God. One blessing."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.4              | NET         | "For he was crucified by reason of weakness, but he lives because of God's power. For we also are weak in him, but we will live together with him, because of God's power" — Christ's weakness (cross) led to power (resurrection). WEB: "he was crucified through weakness, yet he lives through the power of God." The pattern for apostolic ministry.
v.5              | KJV         | "examine yourselves"
v.5              | ESV         | Both equivalent. — Greek "heautous peirazete" — test yourselves, put yourselves on trial. Active, deliberate self-assessment.
v.5              | KJV         | "reprobates"
v.5              | ESV         | "fail to meet the test!" — Greek "adokimoi" — unapproved, failing the test. Those who examine themselves and find Christ is NOT in them.
v.5              | NET         | "Put yourselves to the test to see if you are in the faith; examine yourselves!" — "examine" (dokimazete = test/prove/assay — metallurgical language). WEB: "Test your own selves, whether you are in the faith." Self-examination before accusing others.
v.9              | KJV         | "your perfection"
v.9              | ESV         | "is what we pray" — Greek "katartisin" — restoration, completion, being made complete. Paul's desire: their full maturity.
v.11             | KJV         | "Be perfect"
v.11             | ESV         | "Aim for restoration" — Greek "katartizesthe" — be restored, be mended, be made complete. The same word used for mending nets (Matthew 4:21).
v.11             | NET         | "Aim for restoration, heed my appeal, agree with one another, live in peace" — "aim for restoration" (katartizesthe = be mended/be put in order). WEB: "Be perfected." KJV: "Be perfect." Not sinless perfection but wholeness/maturity.
v.12             | NET         | "Greet one another with a holy kiss" — "holy kiss" (philEmati hagiO) — a standard greeting in the early church, distinguishing Christian fellowship. WEB: "Greet one another with a holy kiss." Cultural form; the principle is warm, genuine affection among believers.
v.14             | KJV         | "the communion of the Holy Ghost"
v.14             | ESV         | "of God and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit be" — Greek "koinōnia" — fellowship, participation, sharing. The Spirit's gift: intimate fellowship with God and with one another.
v.14             | NET         | "The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ and the love of God and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit be with you all" — the fullest Trinitarian benediction in Scripture. WEB: "The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, God's love, and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit be with you all." Three persons, three gifts: grace, love, fellowship.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term                        | Definition                                                   | Notes
----------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------|------
Examine yourselves          | Active self-assessment: is my faith genuine? Is Christ in me? Am I truly in the faith? Not assumed but tested. | 
Reprobate                   | Failing the test — unapproved. One who examines themselves and finds no evidence of genuine faith. | 
Perfection                  | Greek "katartisis" — completion, restoration, maturity. Not sinless perfection but wholeness and spiritual health. | 
Trinitarian benediction     | The blessing invoking all three persons of the Trinity: grace from Christ, love from the Father, fellowship from the Spirit. | 
Communion of the Holy Ghost | The Spirit's gift of fellowship — intimate participation in God's life and in community with believers. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Examine yourselves, whether ye be in the faith" (v.5). When did you last
   seriously examine your faith? Not assume it — examine it. Is Christ in you?
   Is there evidence of genuine spiritual life? Don't coast on past decisions
   — examine present reality.

2. "We can do nothing against the truth, but for the truth" (v.8). Truth is
   unstoppable. You can fight it, deny it, suppress it — but you cannot defeat
   it. Are you on the side of truth or fighting against it?

3. "Be perfect, be of good comfort, be of one mind, live in peace" (v.11).
   Five final commands: be complete, be encouraged, be unified, live peacefully.
   Which of these does your community most need right now?

4. "The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, and the love of God, and the communion
   of the Holy Ghost" (v.14). Three gifts from three persons: grace, love,
   fellowship. Which do you most need to experience today?
5. Paul says "examine yourselves" (v.5) — not "examine your pastor" or
   "examine your church." Self-examination is the priority. What evidence of
   genuine faith do you see in your life? What concerns you?

6. "Know ye not your own selves, how that Jesus Christ is in you?" (v.5). The
   normal Christian experience: Christ IN you. Do you have assurance of
   salvation? What gives you confidence that Christ is in you?

7. Paul's final commands (v.11) include "be of one mind" and "live in peace."
   Unity and peace are not optional extras — they are commands. Where is there
   disunity or conflict in your relationships that needs to be addressed?

8. The Trinitarian benediction (v.14) is the most common blessing used in
   churches today. Do you receive it as a genuine prayer or as a ritual
   closing? What would it mean to truly experience grace, love, and fellowship
   from the Trinity?

9. 2 Corinthians ends where it began — with grace (1:2, 13:14). The letter
   is bookended by grace. How has grace been the theme of your life this year?
   Where do you need more grace?
